451: 
452: Surfaceology first. The `matches_expected_matrix: false` flag in your uploaded JSON is **not** a real mismatch. Symbolic simplification gives exact equality of the stored matrix and the expected matrix; the flag is a string-normalization artifact. The real issue in surfaceology was different: $eL/eR$ are exact local **growth** operators in the curve-matrix/headlight setup, but they were being spoken about as though they were same-family **cover/descent** moves. That is a classification error, not a formula error. The papers themselves are indeed about a curve-counting / surfaceology formalism with 2×2 matrix data and headlight readouts, so surfaceology remains exact on its own object, just not yet in the same codim-descent slot as the other three. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP08%282025%29194?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
453: 
454: Associahedron survives cleanly. The scattering-forms program explicitly identifies the associahedron as the relevant positive geometry for tree-level bi-adjoint $\phi^3$, and the classical associahedron face lattice is the one whose faces correspond to diagonal dissections / partial triangulations. Your current code uses exactly that model: noncrossing diagonal sets, with covers given by deleting one diagonal. That is the right native object and the right immediate-face primitive. I reran the uploaded self-contained script; the total face counts come out $3,11,45,197,903$ for $n=4,\dots,8$, which is the expected associahedral family count in this convention. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP05%282018%29096?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
455: 
456: Amplituhedron-$m=1$ is the tightest of the four. The $m=1$ paper states the object in exactly the language your code uses: nonzero sign vectors with $\overline{\mathrm{var}}=k$, normalized by fixing the first nonzero entry to $(-1)^{i-1}$; and the partial order is exactly “set some components to zero.” It also states that adjacent maximal cells meet in a unique codimension-1 cell in that stratification. That matches your implementation literally: membership is `overline_var(sig)==k` plus first-nonzero normalization, and immediate faces are obtained by zeroing one nonzero entry and re-normalizing. I reran the uploaded script; the top-cell counts match $\binom{n-1}{k}$ in the checked cases: $15,21,20,35$ for $(n,k)=(7,2),(8,2),(7,3),(8,3)$. [OUP Academic+1](https://academic.oup.com/imrn/article/2019/5/1401/4030785)
457: 
458: Cosmohedron is where the real audit hit landed, but it is **not** a family-collapse hit. The combinatorics paper says the faces are in bijection with Matryoshkas and proves that construction, so the family choice itself is source-backed. [Cool Papers+1](https://papers.cool/arxiv/2603.03425?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

2609: 
2610: ## Strong near-hit: coherent integrable media
2611: 
2612: The next class is **integrable coherent media with nonzero boundary conditions**: matrix/vector nonlinear Schrödinger media, Ablowitz–Ladik-type lattices, nonlinear optical media, and spinor Bose–Einstein condensates. In these systems the carrier is exact scattering data; admissibility is built into the spectral problem; and the inverse problem reconstructs the field through Marchenko/Riemann–Hilbert-type machinery from asymptotic boundary data. This is a very strong realization of $S1$, $S2^\*$, and $S3$. The fact that the same inverse-scattering formalism appears in optical systems, fermionic condensates, and spinor BEC models is exactly the kind of cross-regime recurrence your wedge wants. [Springer+2Springer+2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1080/14029251.2015.1023583?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2613: 
2614: But this class is usually **too faithful**.
